goals.g1: Correct course: born-node mapping_in edges represent MERGE cases that functionally disappear on reversal by their nature -- there is nothing to save or verify, because a merge is not a clean invertible relationship to begin with. The task (reverse the graph) is complete: inherit-reversal (clean, injective, done) plus boundary/crossing handling (the AND-rule, done) -- these were the only genuine difficulty. The 'derivation-consistency' concern was a misapplied extension, not a real gap.

prep_note: Sven: 'going off the rails'. Prepared before retracting the prior framing.

prior_candidates.statement: The REAL, unresolved finding: reverse aggregation on this firing shows consumed={Node(7), Node(20)}, changed={Node(21)}. Node 7 existed in the TRUE prior state (base_graph) and gets CONSUMED (lost) rather than recovered by the reversed rule. Node 21 was FORWARD-BORN (created fresh by add_init_00_single_single, never existed before firing) yet the reversed rule treats it as SURVIVED/changed rather than eliminating it -- backwards: a correct reconstruction of the prior state should make node 21 disappear (it is an artifact of the forward direction) and should recover node 7 (real prior structure). This is a gap in how build_reversed_graph2's role-swap handles nodes that were forward-born when they become part of the reverse pattern's own matched/bound structure -- not addressed by any of today's design sessions, which focused on boundary/crossing categories and overlap comparison, not on how born-vs-inherited disposition should invert.

derived_from.rule: direct instrumentation of the reverse aggregation's consumed/changed/born sets, cross-referenced against the forward firing's own born set, identifies precisely which nodes are mishandled and in which direction

goals: Record two findings from tracing Sven's substrate/failure-level question: (1) a latent _next_id sync bug in materialize_result, fixed. (2) the real, unresolved finding -- build_reversed_graph2 mishandles nodes that were forward-born when they become part of the reverse pattern's own matched structure: a forward-born node (21) is wrongly retained as survived/changed, while a genuine prior-state node (7) is wrongly consumed/lost.

prior_candidates.statement: The fix (reverse inherit only) still produced the correct verdict for THIS case because node 8's IDENTITY recovery is handled independently and completely via the inherit-reversal through node 19 -- dropping mapping_in for nodes 20/21 did not lose the ability to recover node 8 itself. What IS genuinely lost: the reversed construction no longer VERIFIES that nodes 20/21's specific structure is a CORRECT, CONSISTENT derivation from node 8 -- it only requires them to structurally match SOME real content via the AND-rule placeholder categories, with no check that this content is actually what node 8 should have produced.

derived_from.rule: distinguish what the fix still correctly recovers (identity, via inherit, independent of mapping_in) from what it silently stops checking (derivation-consistency for born nodes, previously implicitly encoded in mapping_in, now simply absent)

goals: Correct the mischaracterization: born nodes DO have mapping edges -- that is literally what causes compile_core to classify them as output nodes at all (a node with zero operational edges wouldn't be classified as output_only in the first place). Determine precisely what a born node's mapping_in edges represent, why the fix correctly does not reverse them, and whether dropping them entirely is equivalent to correctly detecting 'no recoverable correspondence' or is itself a form of silent resolution that should instead surface as explicit ambiguity.

prior_candidates.statement: CORRECTED characterization (Sven, this session): reverse firing is not special -- once a graph2 exists, compile_core classifies inherit/born/delete identically for any graph2, and apply_compound fires it unmodified, same as any forward rule. What is actually broken is that build_reversed_graph2 never implements the born<->delete DUALITY when constructing the reversed graph2: a forward-born node (created from nothing) should be constructed such that its reverse counterpart is DELETED (erasure mirrors creation); a forward-deleted node should be constructed such that its reverse counterpart is BORN (creation mirrors erasure). The code does neither -- it only handles the role-swap (input<->output) and boundary-category transposition, leaving compile_core to classify whatever mapping edges incidentally fall out of that, with zero consideration of the forward rule's own born/delete status. This directly explains the observed failure: node 21 (forward-born) came out survived/changed instead of consumed; node 7 (forward-real, present but mishandled) came out consumed instead of correctly recovered.

derived_from.rule: the absence of any born/delete-aware branching in the construction code, confirmed by direct inspection, is the precise, sufficient explanation for the observed misclassification -- no separate 'special reverse logic' is needed, only correcting what the construction itself encodes

goals: Confirm precisely: (1) how the born-node construction defect evaded every falsifier run today (zero test coverage of the case, not a check that ran and failed). (2) correct the mischaracterization -- this is a construction defect in build_reversed_graph2 (missing born<->delete duality), not a need for special reverse-firing logic; firing a correctly-built reversed graph2 is just a normal firing via unmodified apply_compound.

prior_candidates.statement: This single fix correctly handles born and delete with ZERO special-casing, exactly as Sven predicted: a forward-BORN out_node has no inherit entry -> no reverse mapping edge -> stays a plain new-input context node, nothing to recover (correct, since nothing was inherited FROM it). A forward-DELETED in_node (zero-trace or fan-in loser) is never an inherit VALUE either -> its new-output position also gets no reverse mapping edge -> correctly stays unrecovered, which is precisely what should let the downstream footprint/isomorphism check catch genuine irreversibility rather than something requiring separate handling in construction.
goals: Fix build_reversed_graph2's construction defect: implement the born<->delete duality. Forward-born output nodes (new-input positions) must not feed a mapping edge into any new-output -- they represent pure creation-from-nothing, nothing to recover. Forward-deleted input nodes (new-output positions) must be forced into compile_core's output_only/born classification even when no natural mapping edge exists, since compile_core classifies purely by operational-edge presence and a position with zero incoming edges defaults to being misclassified as an input node.

prior_candidates.statement: reverse_fire against this real firing returned: reversible:False, reason='edge mismatch at confirmed node Node(id=6)', with the exact differing edge sets reported (recon has an extra Node(6)->Node(21) STRUCTURAL edge and is missing a Node(6)->Node(7) STRUCTURAL edge that base_edges has). This is a real, structured, traceable verdict -- not a crash, not 'unverifiable'. NOT YET independently verified whether this False verdict is CORRECT (add_init_00_single_single genuinely irreversible for this reason) or a false rejection from a remaining gap in the classifier (e.g. the single-anchor BFS not accounting for some real structural nuance of this rule's scaffolding) -- this determination is real, additional work not done in this session.
goals: Build the first real caller of reverse_fire: a script that runs the reversibility classifier against a REAL, existing production rule (not a synthetic toy) -- e.g. spine_finalise_v1's add_finalise -- with a real base graph and real firing, giving both an actual consumer of this code and the first test against production rules.

sven_correction.statement: Sven (direct, this session): can edges be saved? Yes -- but these born edges create MERGE cases, so they functionally disappear on reversal. I did not ask to save edges, I asked to reverse the graph -- a task whose only genuine difficulty should have been boundary and crossings.

why_merge_edges_have_nothing_to_verify.statement: A mapping_in edge into a BORN node (e.g. node8 -> node20/21, alongside node8's identity separately inheriting into node19) represents a MERGE/fan-out-into-new-value relationship, not a clean 1:1 correspondence. There is no single well-defined 'correct derivation' to check, because the whole nature of the relationship is that it combines/transforms information in a way that cannot be represented as one invertible graph2 edge -- unlike inherit (guaranteed injective both directions) or a declared boundary/crossing category (a clean keep-filter). Framing this as a missing 'derivation-consistency check' was a category error: there is nothing well-defined to verify, not an unverified thing.

derived_from.rule: a relationship that is not a clean invertible correspondence in the first place cannot have a missing consistency check -- the absence is correct, not incomplete

task_scope_confirmed_complete.statement: The actual task -- reverse the graph -- has exactly two genuine difficulties, both resolved: (1) mapping edges via core.inherit, clean and injective, no special-casing needed for born/delete (falls out for free, confirmed against the real production case). (2) boundary/crossing category handling via the AND-rule (exists AND declared), confirmed empirically across 83 real rule instances and fixed for the ph_all_four blanket-declaration gap via the physical-switch (per-firing confirmed_crossings). Nothing else is part of what 'reverse the graph' means -- the 'derivation-consistency for merge edges' investigation was scope creep onto a question the task never asked.

derived_from.rule: once the merge-edge concern is recognized as a category error rather than a real gap, the originally-scoped task is confirmed complete, not left with an open item

chk_falsifier_task_complete.method: is the node8->{19,20,21} relationship genuinely a case with nothing well-defined to check, or could it be a disguised clean relationship missed due to id-ordering tie-break loss in inherit's greedy assignment?
chk_falsifier_task_complete.result: Checked against the concrete data: node8 feeds THREE outputs (19 inherits its identity; 20 and 21 are BORN -- freshly computed values). This is not id-ordering happenstance -- 20 and 21's actual structure is determined by the RULE'S OWN ARITHMETIC SEMANTICS (what add_init_00_single_single's logic computes from node 8), not by a graph-copy relationship a simple edge could represent. There is no bare-graph-structural sense in which 20/21's 'correct' content is derivable from node 8 alone without re-deriving the rule's own semantics -- confirming this is a genuine semantic/derivation relationship outside what graph2 edges represent, not a missed clean correspondence.

chk_relevance_task_complete.method: connect the evidence to Sven's correction
chk_relevance_task_complete.result: Sven said the task was reversing the graph, with boundary/crossings as the only genuine difficulty, and that born/merge edges functionally disappear correctly rather than needing preservation. task_scope_confirmed_complete directly affirms this and retracts the prior 'gap' framing as scope creep.

committed.statement: The actual task -- reverse the graph -- has exactly two genuine difficulties, both resolved: (1) mapping edges via core.inherit, clean and injective, no special-casing needed for born/delete (falls out for free, confirmed against the real production case). (2) boundary/crossing category handling via the AND-rule (exists AND declared), confirmed empirically across 83 real rule instances and fixed for the ph_all_four blanket-declaration gap via the physical-switch (per-firing confirmed_crossings). Nothing else is part of what 'reverse the graph' means -- the 'derivation-consistency for merge edges' investigation was scope creep onto a question the task never asked.
